findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 2

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 15

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 7

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 19 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market opportunity, but investors will want to know if real customers actually want this solution—add a slide showing 3-4 specific conversations you've had with villa developers or building managers about their current pain with solar hot water systems, ideally with quotes like "We lose 40% of installations to overheating damage" or "Our maintenance costs are triple what we budgeted." Even informal discovery counts; what matters is showing you've validated that the problem you're solving is keeping people up at night.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit + Solar hot water mandated by regulation

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add a specific regulatory deadline or enforcement date (like "Estidama compliance required by Q3 2025 for all new villa permits") and tie it directly to when developers will actually need your solution, so investors see this isn't just a nice-to-have but a forced buying moment happening right now.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel); Solar hot water mandated by regulation; Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works: for instance, "HelioHelp ingests building orientation, climate zone, and hot water demand, then outputs a bill-of-materials and installation sequence—reducing design time from 3 weeks to 2 hours." This shows investors the actual intelligence layer, not just what it claims to do.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a brief section to your Technology slide listing the key infrastructure you're building on—for example, "HelioHelp runs on [cloud provider], integrates with [weather/mapping APIs], and uses [specific ML frameworks]"—so investors understand what external dependencies exist and that you've thought through vendor lock-in or API reliability risks. This doesn't need to be long, just enough to show you're aware of the operational reality behind the AI layer.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, GCC) with mandated solar installations and utility partnerships, but your deck doesn't mention data security, system certifications, or compliance frameworks that buyers will require before writing checks. Add a single bullet to Slide 5 (Technology) under HelioPro stating something like "ISO 50001 energy management certified + GDPR-compliant installer data platform" — this signals you've thought through the operational reality of enterprise deployments and removes a question investors will ask in the room.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions sheet that shows the math behind your projections: for example, "2026: 50 units at $6K avg hardware revenue + $2.3K licensing per customer = $413K total" and "2027-2030: 15% YoY unit growth driven by ALDAR pipeline (100-500 units) + EtihadWE retrofit (500 units) scaling into base case." Right now investors see your $6.78M 2030 base case but have no way to reverse-engineer whether you're assuming 200 units or 2,000 units, or if your 45% gross margin assumes manufacturing scale you haven't detailed

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: $350K seed funding: 18-month cash runway

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand if your unit economics actually work at scale—right now it looks like you're showing product margins, not customer profitability. Add a simple table showing: average deal size per customer segment (hotels vs. villas), fully-loaded CAC including sales/install costs, lifetime revenue per customer (hardware + O&M contracts over 5-7 years), and the resulting LTV:CAC ratio—for example, if a villa customer costs $8K to acquire but generates $35K over their lifetime, that's a healthy 4.4

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted on Slide 12 is your locked-in near-term revenue, but Slide 14's $300K 2026 hardware projection suggests you're modeling an additional $87K in deals beyond what's currently signed—clarify this gap by adding a line to Slide 14 that breaks 2026 revenue into "Contracted: $213K" and "Pipeline conversion target: $87K" so investors see exactly what you're counting on and from where.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X to expand Ras Al Khaimah facility," "Sales & partnerships: $X for developer relationships," "Working capital: $X for inventory ahead of Q2 2026 Rixos deployment") with specific dollar amounts and timelines tied to your milestones. Right now investors see your $350K seed runway and your 2026 revenue targets, but they don't know what *this* round is for or how it bridges the gap between where you are and those targets.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: EBITDA positive 2027

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets stating the payment status—something like "Rixos (live project, revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, $X paid to date)"—so investors immediately see these aren't pilots but actual paying customers generating real cash. Right now it reads like they're just live installations, not proof of revenue.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows revenue potential and partnerships, but investors need to see actual growth momentum—add specific numbers showing how your revenue or units sold have grown month-over-month or year-over-year (for example, "January: $50K revenue → February: $75K → March: $120K" or "Q1 2024: 200 units installed → Q2 2024: 450 units"). This proves the model is working, not just theoretically sound.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to the "A Year of Performance" section: something like "System uptime: 99.2% | Customer satisfaction: NPS 8/10" or whatever your actual numbers are. This turns a case study into proof that customers stick around and love what you've built, which is exactly what investors need to see before they bet on your scaling model.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project), EtihadWE

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market—replace "Legacy Solar," "Heat Pumps," and "Electric Storage" with real product names or companies (e.g., "Sunrun Solar Systems," "Daikin Heat Pumps," "Tesla Powerwall"). Investors know these categories exist; what they need to see is that you've done the homework on who you're actually displacing and why customers would switch from them specifically to SolarisKit.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why you're better than existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a regional solar giant decides to build exactly what you're building—they have distribution, capital, and brand trust you don't yet. Add a new section called "Why We Win Long-Term" that explains 2-3 defensible moats: for example, "Our patent on the polymer absorber design for flat-roof integration gives us 18 months before competitors can legally enter, during which we're capturing 40% of the GCC market" or "Our exclusive partnership with [local installer network] locks in 60% of qualified installers before incumbents

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors confident in execution—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas like manufacturing scale-up, regulatory/certification, or distribution channels in your target markets. For example, if you're scaling solar thermal in the Middle East and Australia, naming someone like "Dr. [Name], former VP Manufacturing at [relevant solar company], advising on production optimization" or "Sarah Chen, ex-Head of Middle East Operations at [energy company], board member" would show you've thought strategically about who you need in the room.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" alongside his bio. Investors need to see that you're all locked in for the long haul, and this small detail signals you've thought through alignment and retention from day one.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and mention compliance focus in your use of funds, but investors in regulated markets need to see your full regulatory roadmap upfront—not buried. Add a dedicated compliance slide before your financials that maps which UAE/GCC building codes (Estidama, Dubai Municipality, Barjeel, RAK) your system already meets, which certifications you're pursuing by when, and how this unlocks each customer segment (hotels vs. utilities vs. developers each have different approval paths). For example: "Estidama compliance achieved Q1 2026 → unlocks Abu Dhabi developer pipeline (ALDAR, Masdar City); Dubai Municipality approval Q2 2

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated legal compliance slide before the financials section that covers your patent status, regulatory certifications (Solar Keymark, Dubai/Abu Dhabi compliance), and any pending approvals—this isn't just defensive, it's a competitive asset that investors will want to see clearly documented. For example: "Patents: US, EU, GB granted (HelioFlow collector); HelioHelp trademark filed Feb 2026 | Certifications: Solar Keymark certified; Dubai Municipality & Abu Dhabi compliance validated | Regulatory: No pending litigation or IP disputes; all manufacturing meets UAE standards" — this turns your IP strength into a trust signal rather than leaving it scattered across multiple slides.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between these figures and the stated '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' cannot be confirmed as mathematically consistent from the provided text.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing the math: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M opportunity" so investors can instantly verify the calculation rather than having to work it out themselves—this builds confidence that your sizing is rigorous, not rounded.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and duplicate slide content suggests formatting issues
findings.suggestion: Go through the deck systematically and fix every incomplete phrase and placeholder — start with Slide 6 where "Strong push for dec" cuts off (should read "Strong push for decarbonisation"), replace all [URL_REDACTED] instances with actual links or remove them entirely, and delete the duplicate slide content (you have the cover slide, Slide 4, Slide 6, Slide 7, Slide 11, and Slide 15 appearing twice). Run a final spell-check pass — investors notice these details and they undermine the polish of your otherwise strong story.
